claim: Always plan before writing code: start with pen and paper to define purpose, problem, and solution before touching any IDE or LLM
actionable: For your next skill/project, spend 15 minutes on paper defining the problem statement, desired output structure, and high-level approach before prompting Claude Code
source_title: RAW Agentic Coding: ZERO to Agent SKILL

claim: The pivot file (skill.md) is the central organizing point—everything else (tools, prompts, cookbook) branches from it and should reference it
actionable: When building a skill, start by writing skill.md with clear purpose, when to use, variables, and workflow. Make it the single source of truth
source_title: RAW Agentic Coding: ZERO to Agent SKILL

claim: Progressive disclosure: structure skill documentation so agents only see details relevant to their current task via conditional instructions in the cookbook
actionable: Create a cookbook directory with use-case-specific markdown files (e.g., CLI_command.md, claude_code.md) and reference them conditionally in skill.md
source_title: RAW Agentic Coding: ZERO to Agent SKILL

claim: Always have agents run --help before executing unfamiliar commands to ground their understanding in actual CLI documentation
actionable: Add an instruction like 'Before executing [command], run [command] --help to understand its options' in your cookbook
source_title: RAW Agentic Coding: ZERO to Agent SKILL

claim: Use variables and enable flags in skills to allow flexible switching between models/modes without rewriting prompts
actionable: Define a variables section in skill.md with enable_gemini_cli, enable_codex_cli, etc., and use them in conditional workflows
source_title: RAW Agentic Coding: ZERO to Agent SKILL

claim: Create a fork summary prompt template to compress context from one agent conversation and hand it off to a new agent with history preserved
actionable: Build a YAML template in your cookbook with fields for history, summarized_user_prompt, and next_user_request to enable context forking
source_title: RAW Agentic Coding: ZERO to Agent SKILL

claim: Use information-dense keywords (e.g., 'astral UV', 'subprocess', 'interactive terminal') when instructing agents to trigger relevant domain knowledge
actionable: When prompting agents to use specific tools, reference exact tool names and recent frameworks rather than generic descriptions
source_title: RAW Agentic Coding: ZERO to Agent SKILL

claim: Start with proof of concept (V0) not minimum viable product—verify the core idea works before adding refinements
actionable: Build the simplest working version that demonstrates the core functionality, test it, then commit before iterating
source_title: RAW Agentic Coding: ZERO to Agent SKILL

claim: When agents misbehave or produce wrong output, first assume it's your prompt/instructions, not the model's fault
actionable: Before blaming the model, clarify your instructions: make keywords more explicit, add examples, or restructure the conditional logic
source_title: RAW Agentic Coding: ZERO to Agent SKILL

claim: The core four (context, model, prompt, tools) are the primitives—master these and you master agentic engineering in 2025-2026
actionable: When designing skills or agents, always return to these four elements and ensure each is optimized independently
source_title: RAW Agentic Coding: ZERO to Agent SKILL

claim: Use fresh context windows for specialized tasks: instead of one bloated agent, spin up dedicated agents per problem with focused scope
actionable: For complex workflows, fork new terminal/agent sessions with specific scopes rather than overloading one agent with too many tasks
source_title: RAW Agentic Coding: ZERO to Agent SKILL

claim: Avoid magic in automation: keep instructions prescriptive and simple for V0, not trying to be too clever or DRY at first
actionable: Write clear, repetitive instructions in your cookbook rather than abstracting too early; refactor after proving it works
source_title: RAW Agentic Coding: ZERO to Agent SKILL
